text.title: Negotiation and Persuasion Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules grounding negotiation. Each rule pairs a binary check + sourced rationale.

rule.statement: Prep brief MUST contain a concrete BATNA (named alternative + its value). 'I'll walk away' is not a BATNA.
rule.rationale: Without a concrete BATNA the author negotiates from fear, not options.

rule.statement: Prep brief MUST estimate the ZOPA (zone of possible agreement) by overlapping each party's reserve point. If ZOPA is negative, the deal is impossible — change scope or walk.
rule.rationale: Negotiating without ZOPA estimation wastes both parties' time.

rule.statement: Brief MUST map interests (the why) for both parties separately from positions (the what). Positions-only briefs miss creative options.
rule.rationale: Positions are zero-sum by construction; interests often allow mutual-gain bundling.

rule.statement: Brief MUST cite objective criteria (market rate, third-party benchmark, prior agreement) for each term. 'Because I want it' is forbidden.
rule.rationale: Objective criteria anchor the conversation in shared facts; without them it devolves into willpower contest.

rule.statement: Persuasion levers (reciprocity, commitment, social proof, authority, liking, scarcity) MUST be tagged honestly. Manipulative use (fake scarcity, fake authority) is forbidden.
rule.rationale: Manipulative use destroys trust on detection; honest use surfaces real reasons to agree.
